blurb: Colorado's native climbing clematis: nodding lavender-blue bells scramble through foothill shrubs in spring, followed by silky seed plumes. A cold-hardy, well-behaved native alternative to the big garden clematis.
sun: Part shade to part sun — a foothills woodland-edge plant
water: Low to moderate; xeric once established
spread: Twining/scrambling climber (clings by its leaf-stalks); clumping root, not running
seasons: Nodding lavender-blue bells in spring; divided foliage through summer; silky seed plumes after bloom; deciduous in winter
wildlife: Early-season nectar; seed plumes and cover for birds and insects
deer: Generally avoided — clematis foliage is acrid
toxic: Like all clematis, contains protoanemonin — foliage and sap are irritant and toxic if eaten raw; wash hands after handling. NOT the CO List B orange-peel clematis (Clematis orientalis)

shots.cap: Spring — nodding lavender bells on the climbing vine · © tmr3 (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Spring — the nodding bell flowers up close · © Kara Carragher (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist
Summer — divided blue-green foliage · © patcooper (CC-BY-NC) / iNaturalist

care.hardiness: A high-elevation Rocky Mountain native (it ranges across Montana, Colorado and the interior West), so it is thoroughly cold-hardy — comfortably through Boulder's USDA zone 5b–6a winters; cold is never the limiting factor.[1][2]
care.planting: Plant in spring after the mid-May last frost, or in early fall, at a woodland edge or the base of a shrub or low trellis it can scramble up. It naturally grows in the dappled light and leaf-litter of foothill thickets, so give it a partly shaded root run; in pots use a gritty, free-draining mix and shade the container, but it is happiest planted out where its roots can range.[1]
care.sun: Part shade to part sun — a foothills woodland-edge plant rather than a full-sun climber. Morning sun with afternoon shade, or high dappled light, suits it best on the Front Range; shading the roots while the top reaches into light is the usual clematis rule.[1]
care.soil: Adaptable, growing in dry to moist, well-drained soils; it tolerates Boulder's lean, alkaline clay-loam as long as it drains and is never waterlogged. Mulch or a leaf-litter layer over the root zone mimics its native woodland-edge habitat.[1]
care.water: Low to moderate; drought-tolerant once established, in keeping with its dry-to-moist mountain habitat. Water new plants through their first season or two to establish, then taper — it does not want a soggy site.[1]
care.spacing: Give it 3–6 ft of lateral room; it climbs and scrambles 6–10 ft by twisting its leaf-stalks around twigs of host shrubs rather than running at the root. A single plant will lace through an open shrub or a small trellis without overwhelming it — unlike its aggressive cousin western virgin's bower.[1]
care.propagation: Grow from seed or from softwood/semi-ripe cuttings; layering also works, since pegged stems root at the nodes. Seed needs a long cold-moist stratification — sow in fall and let it overwinter outdoors, or give roughly 60–180 days of cold-moist chilling at 33–40°F before spring sowing; germination is slow.[1]
care.sow: Sow ripe seed in fall in a protected outdoor bed or pot so it gets natural winter chilling, or sow stratified seed in spring. Be patient — germination is slow and uneven.[1]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed for about 60–180 days at 33–40°F (a fall outdoor sowing accomplishes this naturally) to break dormancy before it will germinate.[1]
care.bloom: Nodding, solitary lavender-blue to purple bells in spring, roughly May into July, followed by silky, plumed seedheads that catch the light through summer — it flowers on the previous season's wood.[1]
care.maintenance: Low. Because it blooms in spring on old (previous-season) wood, prune only lightly and only just after flowering — tidy or shorten stems then, never with a hard late-winter cutback, which would remove the wood that carries the spring flowers. Let the ornamental seed plumes stand; its foliage dies back in winter and breaks again from the woody base.[1][3]
care.troubles: Largely trouble-free in our dry air; the main pitfall is a too-wet or too-sunny, hot site. Like other clematis its sap can irritate skin, so wear gloves when cutting it back (see 'Edible parts'). Note this is the well-behaved native clematis — do NOT confuse it with Clematis orientalis, the yellow orange-peel clematis, a CO List B noxious weed.[3]

edible.summary: Like all clematis, Rocky Mountain clematis is toxic — no part is edible, and the bruised sap irritates skin.[3][4]
edible.caution: All parts contain protoanemonin, an acrid irritant glycoside released when the tissue is bruised or chewed. Eating it causes intense burning and pain in the mouth, salivation, vomiting, and diarrhea; the same compound makes the fresh sap a skin and mucous-membrane irritant, so handling cut stems can cause redness and a burning sensation — wear gloves when pruning. It is toxic to dogs, cats, and horses as well as people; keep children and pets from chewing it. (Drying largely destroys protoanemonin, but never rely on that — treat the whole plant as not for consumption.)[3][4]
